schools.summary: The LORD speaking from the tabernacle in the wilderness of Sinai: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation, on the first day of the second month, in the second year after they were come out of the land of Egypt, saying' — the setting — 'the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation': the book opening with God speaking to Moses from the tabernacle now reared up, in the wilderness, to order and number his people (the witnesses noting the significance — the precise time and place, the wilderness of Sinai, the tabernacle of the congregation, the first day of the second month of the second year, a month after the tabernacle was set up, Exodus 40:17, the lesson — God speaking from the tabernacle, the place of his dwelling and his oracle, the voice from the mercy seat, the God who dwells among his people now ordering them, Numbers 7:89, the figure — the God of order who numbers and arranges his people for the journey, the host of the LORD set in array, the careful dating marking the historical reality and the new stage, the people now to be mustered and marshalled for the march to Canaan, the figure of God who dwells among his people and orders all their goings, the tabernacle the center from which he commands); the wilderness and the tabernacle (God's dwelling now reared, the place of the oracle, Numbers 7:89) — the setting (God speaking from his dwelling); the precise dating (a month after the tabernacle was set up, Exodus 40:17) — the figure (the new stage, the people ordered for the march, the providence, God ordering his people from the tabernacle) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the opening setting of Numbers establish?

sides.position: God speaking from the tabernacle now reared up, in the wilderness of Sinai in the second year (Exodus 40:17), to order and number his people - the God who dwells among his people speaking from his sanctuary (Numbers 7:89), the God of order who marshals his host for the journey to Canaan

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation, on the first day of the second month, in the second year after they were come out of the land of Egypt
the LORD speaking from the tabernacle in the wilderness of Sinai (the crux of God ordering his people from his dwelling)
the God who dwells among his people speaking from his sanctuary to order and number them (Numbers 7:89)
the God of order who marshals his host for the journey (Exodus 40:17)

schools.summary: The command to number the congregation by name: 'take ye the sum of all the congregation of the children of Israel, after their families, by the house of their fathers, with the number of their names, every male by their polls' — Moses to take the sum of the whole congregation, by families and fathers' houses, every male counted by name and head (the take the sum, the numbering, the census, the after their families, by the house of their fathers, the ordered count by tribe and family, the with the number of their names, every male by their polls, each counted individually, by name and head, the figure, the careful numbering of God's people, each counted by name, not a faceless multitude but every man known and reckoned, the truth that God knows his own by name and number, 'he calleth his own sheep by name,' John 10:3, 'the very hairs of your head are all numbered,' Matthew 10:30, the people numbered for the order and the march); the numbering by families and fathers' houses (the ordered census) — the count (the people numbered by tribe); 'with the number of their names, every male by their polls' (each counted by name and head, every man known, John 10:3, the figure, God knowing his own by name, Matthew 10:30, the providence, the people numbered, the men of war to be reckoned, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Take ye the sum of all the congregation of the children of Israel, after their families, by the house of their fathers, with the number of their names, every male by their polls
the command to number the congregation by name
each counted by name, not a faceless multitude but every man known and reckoned
the God who knows his own by name and number (John 10:3; Matthew 10:30)

schools.summary: The numbering of all able to go to war: 'from twenty years old and upward, all that are able to go forth to war in Israel: thou and Aaron shall number them by their armies' — the men of war — 'all that are able to go forth to war in Israel... number them by their armies': the census counted the men of war, from twenty years old and upward, all fit for battle, numbered by their armies (the witnesses noting the significance — the muster of the host, the men able to bear arms, the fighting force of Israel, counted and arranged by their armies for the march and the conquest of Canaan, the lesson — Israel as the host of the LORD, the people not a mere multitude but an army, the armies of the living God, marshalled and numbered for war, the LORD of hosts at their head, the host that went out of Egypt, Exodus 12:41, now mustered for the march, the figure — the people of God as an army, the church militant, called to spiritual warfare, 'endure hardness, as a good soldier of Jesus Christ,' 2 Timothy 2:3, 'put on the whole armour of God,' Ephesians 6:11, the men of war a figure of the soldiers of Christ, the host of God arrayed for battle, the twenty years and upward marking the age of strength and responsibility, the able-bodied called to the warfare, the figure of the people of God mustered as an army under the LORD of hosts); the men of war from twenty years (the fighting force, the able-bodied) — the muster (the army of Israel); 'thou and Aaron shall number them by their armies' (the people arranged by armies, the host marshalled, Exodus 12:41, the figure, the church militant, the soldiers of Christ, 2 Timothy 2:3; Ephesians 6:11, the providence, the host numbered, the princes to assist, vv.4-16) — Clarke, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the numbering of the men of war signify?

sides.position: Israel as the host of the LORD, the able-bodied from twenty years mustered and arranged by their armies (Exodus 12:41) - a figure of the people of God as an army, the church militant called to spiritual warfare under the LORD of hosts (2 Timothy 2:3; Ephesians 6:11)

schools.summary: The people declaring their pedigrees in the numbering: 'and they assembled all the congregation together on the first day of the second month, and they declared their pedigrees after their families, by the house of their fathers, according to the number of the names, from twenty years old and upward, by their polls' — the declaring of pedigrees — 'they declared their pedigrees after their families, by the house of their fathers': the whole congregation assembled, and each declared his pedigree, his descent by family and father's house, so that the count was ordered by genealogy (the witnesses noting the significance — the careful ordering of the people by descent, each man owning his family and tribe, his place in Israel established by his pedigree, the count not a faceless mass but an ordered register of families, the lesson — the importance of the genealogies in Israel, the descent that established each man's place, his tribe, his inheritance, and for the priests and Levites their service, and through the line of Judah the descent of the Messiah, the careful keeping of the pedigrees, 'all Israel were reckoned by genealogies,' 1 Chronicles 9:1, the figure — the order of God's people, each known by name and family and set in his place, and a figure of the spiritual genealogy, the question of whose we are, 'born... of God,' John 1:13, the new birth that establishes our place in the family of God, the order and the register of God's people, each owning his descent and his place); the whole congregation assembled (the day of the numbering, vv.1) — the assembly (the people gathered); the declaring of the pedigrees by family and father's house (the ordered count by descent, 1 Chronicles 9:1) — the figure (the order of God's people by genealogy, the providence, the people numbered as commanded, vv.19) — Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the declaring of pedigrees signify?

sides.position: The careful ordering of the people by descent, each man owning his family and place in Israel (1 Chronicles 9:1) - the genealogies that established each man's tribe, inheritance, and service, and through Judah the Messianic line; a figure of the order of God's people and the spiritual descent of those born of God (John 1:13)

the men of war of Simeon numbered, a large tribe here yet later reduced (Numbers 26:14)

schools.summary: The count of Gad begun: 'of the children of Gad, by their generations, after their families, by the house of their fathers, according to the number of the names, from twenty years old and upward, all that were able to go forth to war' — the numbering of Gad, by families and fathers' houses, every male of military age (the of the children of Gad, the tribe numbered, the by their generations, after their families, the ordered count by descent, vv.18, the all that were able to go forth to war, the men of war, vv.3, the figure, Gad numbered in order, the ordered count continuing, the host counted tribe by tribe, each by descent and military age, the strength of Gad reckoned, a warlike tribe, Genesis 49:19; 1 Chronicles 12:8); the numbering of Gad by family and father's house (the ordered count, vv.18) — the count (Gad's men); 'from twenty years old and upward, all that were able to go forth to war' (the men of military age counted, vv.3, the figure, the men of war, the warlike Gad, Genesis 49:19, the providence, the count of Gad, the number to follow, vv.25) — Gill.

themes: Of the children of Gad, by their generations, after their families... all that were able to go forth to war
the count of Gad begun
Gad numbered by descent and military age, a warlike tribe (Genesis 49:19)
the host counted tribe by tribe

schools.summary: The number of the tribe of Gad: 'those that were numbered of them, even of the tribe of Gad, were forty and five thousand six hundred and fifty' — the men of war of Gad numbered 45,650 (the those that were numbered, the count completed, the forty and five thousand six hundred and fifty, the number of Gad's men of war, with the odd fifty noted, the figure, the exact number of Gad's host recorded, even to the fifty, the precise reckoning, the careful exactness of the count, not rounded but exact, the tribe's strength reckoned to the last, the host of the LORD numbered tribe by tribe with exactness); the men of Gad numbered (the count) — the count (Gad's men); 'were forty and five thousand six hundred and fifty' (the number of Gad's men of war, 45,650, the exact count to the fifty, the figure, the precise reckoning, not rounded, the providence, the number recorded, the next tribe, vv.26-27) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: Those that were numbered of them, even of the tribe of Gad, were forty and five thousand six hundred and fifty
the number of the tribe of Gad
the men of war of Gad numbered to the very fifty, the precise count not rounded
the host numbered tribe by tribe with exactness

schools.summary: The count of Judah begun: 'of the children of Judah, by their generations, after their families, by the house of their fathers, according to the number of the names, from twenty years old and upward, all that were able to go forth to war' — the numbering of Judah, by families and fathers' houses, every male of military age (the of the children of Judah, the royal tribe numbered, the by their generations, after their families, the ordered count by descent, vv.18, the all that were able to go forth to war, the men of war, vv.3, the figure, Judah numbered in order, the largest tribe, the royal tribe through whom the Messiah came, Genesis 49:10, the tribe of the scepter, the most numerous of the host, set in the front of the camp, Numbers 2:3, the strength of Judah reckoned, the leading tribe); the numbering of Judah by family and father's house (the ordered count, the royal tribe, Genesis 49:10) — the count (Judah's men); 'from twenty years old and upward, all that were able to go forth to war' (the men of military age counted, vv.3, the figure, the men of war, the leading tribe, Numbers 2:3, the providence, the count of Judah, the number to follow, vv.27) — Gill.

themes: Of the children of Judah, by their generations, after their families... all that were able to go forth to war
the count of Judah begun
Judah numbered by descent and military age, the royal tribe through whom the Messiah came (Genesis 49:10)
the largest and leading tribe of the host (Numbers 2:3)

schools.summary: The number of the tribe of Judah, the largest: 'those that were numbered of them, even of the tribe of Judah, were threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred' — the men of war of Judah numbered 74,600, the largest of all the tribes (the those that were numbered, the count completed, the threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred, 74,600, the greatest number, the figure, Judah the most numerous tribe, the leading and largest of the host, the strength of the royal tribe, the fulfillment of the promise of Judah's pre-eminence, Genesis 49:8-10, the tribe of the scepter the strongest, set in the front of the march, Numbers 2:3-9, the greatest host, fitting the tribe of the Messiah); the men of Judah numbered (the count) — the count (Judah's men); 'were threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred' (the number of Judah's men of war, 74,600, the largest, Genesis 49:8-10, the figure, the pre-eminence of Judah, the royal tribe, the providence, the greatest number, the leading tribe, Numbers 2:3-9) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Those that were numbered of them, even of the tribe of Judah, were threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred
the number of the tribe of Judah, the largest
Judah the most numerous tribe, the fulfillment of Judah's pre-eminence (Genesis 49:8-10)
the strongest host fitting the tribe of the Messiah (Numbers 2:3-9)

schools.summary: Gill: records the tribe of Issachar coming forward to be numbered by their generations, families, and fathers' houses.

themes: Of the children of Issachar, by their generations
enrolled by families and fathers' houses
the orderly numbering of God's people

schools.summary: Gill: the tribe of Issachar numbered fifty and four thousand and four hundred, the men from twenty years old and upward able to go to war.

themes: the tribe of Issachar, were fifty and four thousand and four hundred
the men able to go forth to war
God's faithfulness in multiplying Abraham's seed (Gen. 15:5)

themes: Of the children of Ephraim, by their generations
enrolled by families and fathers' houses
the orderly numbering of God's people

schools.summary: Gill: the tribe of Ephraim numbered forty thousand and five hundred.

Clarke: Ephraim, blessed beyond Manasseh (Gen. 48:20), is increased by thousands more than Manasseh and more than Benjamin, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy that the younger should be greater than the elder (Gen. 48:19); no word of God can fall to the ground.

themes: the tribe of Ephraim, were forty thousand and five hundred
greater than Manasseh
Jacob's prophecy fulfilled (Gen. 48:19)

schools.summary: Gill: records the tribe of Manasseh coming forward to be numbered by their generations, families, and fathers' houses.

themes: Of the children of Manasseh, by their generations
enrolled by families and fathers' houses
the orderly numbering of God's people

schools.summary: Gill: the tribe of Manasseh numbered thirty and two thousand and two hundred, the men from twenty years old and upward able to go to war.

themes: the tribe of Manasseh, were thirty and two thousand and two hundred
the men able to go forth to war
God's faithfulness in multiplying Abraham's seed (Gen. 15:5)

schools.summary: Gill: records the tribe of Benjamin coming forward to be numbered by their generations, families, and fathers' houses.

themes: Of the children of Benjamin, by their generations
enrolled by families and fathers' houses
the orderly numbering of God's people

schools.summary: Gill: the tribe of Benjamin numbered thirty-five thousand four hundred.

Wesley: Benjamin, though he had more immediate sons than any of his brethren (Gen. 46:21), is now among the smallest, while Dan, with but one son (Gen. 46:23), is nearly the largest; God easily makes such great and strange changes in families (1 Sam. 2:5), so let none boast in their numerous offspring.

themes: the tribe of Benjamin, were thirty and five thousand and four hundred
the once-numerous now small
God's strange changes in families (1 Sam. 2:5)

schools.summary: Gill: records the tribe of Dan coming forward to be numbered by their generations, families, and fathers' houses.

themes: Of the children of Dan, by their generations
enrolled by families and fathers' houses
the orderly numbering of God's people

schools.summary: Gill: the tribe of Dan numbered threescore and two thousand and seven hundred, the men from twenty years old and upward able to go to war.

themes: the tribe of Dan, were threescore and two thousand and seven hundred
the men able to go forth to war
God's faithfulness in multiplying Abraham's seed (Gen. 15:5)

the men able to go forth to war
God's faithfulness in multiplying Abraham's seed (Gen. 15:5)

schools.summary: Gill: these are the sums of the numbers of the several tribes given before, numbered by Moses, Aaron, and the twelve princes, each for his fathers' house.

Henry: the total was 600,000 fighting men and 3,550 over; though the Levites may have been reckoned in the earlier total (Exod. 38:26) and were now set apart, so many more had reached twenty years that the number held — to show that whatever we part with for God's service we are no losers by.

themes: these are those that were numbered... the princes of Israel, being twelve men
the great total of fighting men
no losers by what we part with for God

schools.summary: Gill: all the tribes, excepting Levi, put together made the sum total — all the children of Israel from twenty years old and upward, able to go forth to war.

themes: all those that were numbered... from twenty years old and upward
all that were able to go forth to war
the people of the promise become a host in arms

schools.summary: Gill: 603,550 — exactly the number when assessed for the tabernacle about seven months before (Exod. 38:26), so that scarcely one above twenty seemed to have died in that time; a marvel of God's preserving and multiplying his people.

themes: all they that were numbered were six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty
the same as the earlier count
the seventy of Egypt grown into a host (Gen. 46:27)

schools.summary: Gill: Levi was excepted from the muster, being employed in a kind of warfare already, and so not to be engaged in another.

Henry: singular services are recompensed with singular honours; the Levites, who distinguished themselves in the matter of the golden calf (Exod. 32:26), were made guardians of the holy things, the care of the tabernacle committed to them.

themes: the Levites... were not numbered among them
Levi set apart from the warfare of arms
singular service recompensed with singular honour

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD himself ordered Levi not to be numbered, lest it be thought Moses did it of himself out of affection for his own tribe; they were exempted, not forbidden, to war (they might fight when needful, as the Maccabees), but not forced.

themes: For the LORD had spoken unto Moses
the exemption by God's command, not Moses' partiality
a calling to the service of the sanctuary

schools.summary: Gill: Levi was not to be numbered along with the other tribes, though it might be, and afterwards was, numbered by itself.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.49-51 — Levi was not numbered, but appointed over the tabernacle of testimony and all its vessels, to bear and minister to it.

themes: thou shalt not number the tribe of Levi
neither take the sum of them among the children of Israel
Levi's distinct vocation, the sanctuary not the sword

schools.summary: Gill: the tabernacle is called 'of testimony' from the ark within it, in which was the law of God, a testimony of his will to his people.
Ellicott: the testimony denotes the two tables of the law placed in the ark (Exod. 25:16, 21), whence the ark, tabernacle, and tent are called of the testimony.

themes: appoint the Levites over the tabernacle of testimony
they shall bear... minister... and encamp round about
a tribe whose whole life circled God's witness

schools.summary: Gill: the Levites took down and set up the tabernacle whenever the congregation journeyed and took it with them.
Ellicott and Wesley: the 'stranger' (zar) here is not a foreigner but one of another tribe, any not of Levi, who came near to do the Levites' office; he was to be put to death.

themes: the Levites shall take it down... shall set it up
the stranger that cometh nigh shall be put to death
holy things not for unhallowed hands

schools.summary: Gill: the people were ordered into camps, each tribe to its own troop and regiment, every man by his own camp and standard.
Ellicott: there were four standards — Judah, Reuben, Ephraim, and Dan — corresponding to the four camps, each of three tribes, pitched round the tent of meeting (Num. 2:3ff).

themes: every man by his own camp, and every man by his own standard
the four standards about the tent
a model of holy order (1 Cor. 14:33)

schools.summary: Gill: the Levites pitched between the tabernacle and the camps, to guard it and keep the unauthorised from polluting or plundering it, their position like the living creatures round the throne (Rev. 4:6).
Ellicott: 'wrath' (kezeph) denotes some immediate visitation of the hand of God, such as the plague; the Levites' guard kept it from the congregation.

themes: the Levites shall pitch round about the tabernacle
that there be no wrath upon the congregation
the holy guard of the holy place (Rev. 4:6)

schools.summary: Gill: they pitched by their camps and standards, kept their distance from the tabernacle, and meddled not with what was peculiar to the Levites; the repetition 'so did they' shows how readily, punctually, and perfectly they obeyed.

themes: the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses
so did they
the ordered host beginning its journey in obedience
